source: WHO - COVID-19 Advice for the Public
content: COVID-19 spreads mainly through respiratory droplets and aerosols released when an infected person breathes, talks, coughs, or sneezes. Wearing a well-fitted mask in crowded or poorly ventilated spaces helps reduce transmission, especially during outbreaks or high-risk exposure.

Masks can help reduce the spread of COVID-19 by lowering the amount of respiratory virus released into the air and reducing exposure to infectious particles. Layered prevention, including masking, ventilation, testing, and staying home when sick, works better than relying on one step alone.

COVID-19 vaccines are safe and effective and reduce the risk of severe disease, hospitalization, and death. Vaccination remains one of the most important evidence-based ways to limit serious outcomes from COVID-19 infection.

COVID-19 vaccination lowers the chance of severe illness and helps protect people at higher risk of complications. Vaccine side effects are usually mild and temporary, while severe disease from infection can cause much greater harm.

COVID-19 can spread from person to person through close contact and shared air. Improving ventilation, avoiding close contact when unwell, covering coughs, washing hands, and wearing a mask in higher-risk settings can help reduce spread.

Vitamin C supports normal immune function, but it does not prevent COVID-19 infection and is not a proven cure for COVID-19. Claims that supplements alone can stop COVID-19 are not supported by strong clinical evidence.

Public health measures for COVID-19 include respiratory hygiene, hand hygiene, ventilation, vaccination, and staying home when sick. These measures reduce the spread of infection and help protect vulnerable people.
A healthy diet supports overall health, immune function, and long-term disease prevention. No single food or supplement can replace evidence-based treatment for serious illness.

Regular physical activity improves cardiovascular health, supports mental wellbeing, and reduces the risk of chronic disease. Exercise is beneficial for health but should not be presented as a cure for every illness.
Hand washing with soap and water helps remove germs and reduces the spread of many infectious diseases. Proper hand hygiene is a basic but effective public health measure.

Vitamins and minerals are important for normal body function, but high doses do not automatically improve health and may sometimes cause harm. Supplements should not be described as guaranteed treatments for serious disease.

Mental health conditions such as depression and anxiety are real medical conditions that can be treated effectively. Evidence-based care may include therapy, medication, and social support depending on the person's needs.

Depression and anxiety are not signs of weakness and should not be dismissed as something a person can simply overcome by willpower alone. Professional support can make a major difference in recovery.
Mental health affects how people think, feel, and act. Early support, access to care, and reducing stigma help improve outcomes for people experiencing mental health conditions.
